9.text: lru_cache from the standard library provides memoization with automatic eviction. It's the key optimization: the Bedrock CountTokens API has latency (~200ms per call), so caching identical requests avoids redundant network round-trips.

12.text: The entire library revolves around this single class. It encapsulates the Bedrock client, the LRU cache, and both the counting and truncation algorithms. All external interfaces (SDK and CLI) delegate to this class.

16.text: The supported models list is a class-level constant used for validation in _format_input_for_model. Only Anthropic Claude models support the Bedrock CountTokens API — other model families (Titan, Llama, Mistral) use different tokenization approaches.

31.text: The constructor takes an optional region and cache size. Region follows boto3's resolution chain (env var → config file → instance metadata), so most users never need to specify it explicitly.

40.text: _client = None enables lazy initialization — the boto3 client isn't created until the first API call. This means importing the library and creating a counter instance is fast and doesn't require AWS credentials.

43.text: This is the critical caching setup. lru_cache is applied to the bound method _count_tokens_impl at instance creation time. This means each BedrockTokenCounter instance has its own independent cache — important because different instances may target different AWS regions.

48.text: Lazy client initialization: the boto3 client is created on first access, not in __init__. This is a property (not a method) so callers use self.client naturally. The if None check ensures it's created exactly once.

55.text: The Bedrock CountTokens API wraps text in a message structure that adds ~7 tokens of overhead. This method measures that overhead by counting tokens for a single character "A" and subtracting 1. The result is cached via the same LRU cache, so overhead is computed once per model.

61.text: Using "A" (a single ASCII character that tokenizes to exactly 1 token) as the probe text. The raw API returns overhead + 1, so subtracting 1 isolates the message structure cost. This is a clever calibration technique.

66.text: This is the raw API call method — the one that lru_cache wraps. It formats the input, calls Bedrock, and returns the raw token count (including message overhead). The cache key is (text, model_id).

74.text: The actual Bedrock API call. count_tokens is a relatively new Bedrock Runtime API operation. It accepts the same input format as invoke_model but returns only the token count without running inference — much cheaper and faster.

103.text: Model validation happens here, not at the API boundary. This gives users a clear error message listing all supported models, rather than a cryptic Bedrock API error. The check uses a simple list membership test — fast enough since there are only ~12 models.

106.text: The input format mirrors what you'd send to invoke_model: an Anthropic messages-format body wrapped in invokeModel. The max_tokens: 1 is required by the API schema but irrelevant for counting — no inference runs.

122.text: The public count_tokens method. It calls the cached implementation, then subtracts the message overhead so users see only the text's token count. Without this subtraction, "Hello" would report ~8 tokens instead of 1.

139.text: max(0, ...) guards against edge cases where overhead subtraction could produce negative counts (e.g., empty strings). Defensive, but prevents confusing negative token counts.

148.text: Exposes the underlying lru_cache statistics. The hit rate is the key metric: a high hit rate means the truncation algorithm is efficiently reusing previous counts rather than making redundant API calls.

157.text: The truncation algorithm is the most complex part of the library. It finds the longest prefix of text that fits within max_tokens, using an adaptive approach that typically converges in 3-5 API calls. The return_metadata flag exposes internals for debugging.

181.text: Phase 1 begins: count the full text to determine if truncation is needed at all. This uses the cached raw count (including overhead) because the truncation algorithm works with raw counts internally for consistency.

186.text: Early exit: if the text already fits, return it unchanged. This is the common case for short inputs and avoids all the estimation machinery. The overhead-adjusted count is used for the user-facing comparison.

196.text: The initial chars-per-token ratio is computed from the full text. This is the algorithm's starting estimate — it assumes the ratio is roughly uniform across the text. For English prose this is ~4 chars/token; for code it varies more.

202.text: Text analysis heuristics refine the initial estimate. Punctuation-heavy text has more tokens per character (each punctuation mark is often its own token). These adjustments are small (2-5%) but improve first-guess accuracy, saving an API call or two.

217.text: The target character count is the algorithm's first guess at where to cut the text. It multiplies the desired token count by the adjusted chars-per-token ratio. If the heuristics are good, this lands within a few tokens of the target.

224.text: Phase 2: the adaptive convergence loop. Each iteration counts tokens for the current estimate, then adjusts. The 20-call limit prevents runaway loops — in practice, convergence happens in 3-5 iterations for most text.

232.text: Three outcomes per iteration: exact match (done), under target (expand), or over target (shrink). The algorithm uses the chars-per-token ratio to estimate how much to adjust, getting more accurate with each measurement.

250.text: Phase 3: fine-tuning. When the estimated adjustment is ≤5 characters, the algorithm switches from ratio-based jumps to character-by-character scanning. This finds the exact token boundary at the cost of a few more API calls.

268.text: The over-target branch mirrors the under-target logic: estimate how many characters to remove, then switch to character-by-character if close. The asymmetry is that removal scans backward from the current position.

295.text: API errors during the loop trigger a fallback: reduce the target by 10 characters and retry. This handles transient Bedrock throttling without aborting the entire truncation.

301.text: Final verification: re-count the best result to confirm accuracy. If it's not exact and we have API budget remaining, fall back to an exhaustive linear scan. This guarantees correctness at the cost of more calls for edge cases.

306.text: The exhaustive fallback scans from the beginning of the text, checking every prefix length. This is expensive (O(n) API calls) but only triggers when the adaptive algorithm fails to converge — typically for text with highly irregular tokenization patterns.

330.text: The return_metadata path exposes api_calls and final_token_count for debugging and performance monitoring. The CLI could use this with a --verbose flag to show how efficient the truncation was.
